result.markdown:
--- title: "Predicting body temperature distributions from TPC shapes" description: "An exploration of how to predict body temperature distributions from thermal performance curve shapes and microclimate data." date: 2024-03-05 categories: [model] format: html: code-fold: true out-width: "50%" fig-align: "center" fig-dpi: 300 lightbox: true --- ::: {.cell code-description='Setup'} ```{.r .cell-code} # packages library(tidyverse) library(sn) library(kableExtra) ``` ::: ## Context I have been exploring if it is possible to predict the distribution of body temperatures that an ectotherm experiences in a given environment based on the shape of its thermal performance curve (TPC) and microclimate data. Below is a quick exploration of how to do so in R. ## Defining functions First, I define a TPC function based on [Deutsch et al. (2008)](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.0709472105). This function uses Topt, CTmin, and CTmax to calculate the performance of an organism at a given body temperature. These parameters are often estimated from empirical data so it makes the whole thing very easily interpretable. ::: {.cell code-description='Defining TPC function'} ```{.r .cell-code} # define TPC function tpc <- function(temps, topt, ctmin, ctmax) { # initialize performance vector and define psig perf <- rep(NA, length(temps)) psig <- -((ctmin - topt) / 4) # calculate performance for (i in seq_along(temps)){ if (temps[i] < topt) { perf[i] <- exp(-((temps[i] - topt) / (2 * psig))^2) } else { perf[i] <- 1 - ((temps[i] - topt) / (topt - ctmax))^2 } } # correct for negative values and return ifelse(perf < 0, 0, perf) } # example usage temps <- seq(0, 40, by = 0.1) topt <- 25 ctmin <- 10 ctmax <- 35 tpc_values <- tpc(temps, topt, ctmin, ctmax) tibble(temps, tpc_values) |> ggplot(aes(x = temps, y = tpc_values)) + geom_line(linewidth = 2) + labs(x = "Body Temperature (°C)", y = "Performance") + theme_minimal() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/tpc-function-1.png){width=2100} ::: ::: Second, I define a function that generates a skew-normal distribution of body temperatures based on a mean, standard deviation, and skewness parameter. To do this I use two functions: - `cp2dp()` from the `sn` package to convert the mean, sd, and skewness parameters to the location, scale, and shape parameters of the skew-normal distribution. - `dsn()` from the `sn` package to calculate the density of the skew-normal distribution at a given set of body temperatures. ::: {.cell code-description='Defining function to generate body temperature distributions'} ```{.r .cell-code} gen_tb_distrib <- function(temps, mean, sd, skew) { # stop if SD is negative or if skew is outside of -1 to 1 if (sd < 0) stop("SD must be non-negative") if (skew <= -1 || skew >= 1) stop("Skew must be between -0.99 and 0.99") # estimate the parameters of the skew-normal distribution params <- cp2dp(c(mean, sd, skew), "SN") # generate a sequence of body temperatures and calculate the density dsn(temps, xi = params[1], omega = params[2], alpha = params[3]) } # example usage tb_distrib <- gen_tb_distrib( temps = seq(0, 50, by = 0.1), mean = 25, sd = 5, skew = 0.5 ) tibble(temps = seq(0, 50, by = 0.1), density = tb_distrib) |> ggplot(aes(x = temps, y = density)) + geom_line(linewidth = 2) + labs(x = "Body Temperature (°C)", y = "Density") + theme_minimal() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/create-tb-distribs-1.png){width=2100} ::: ::: Third, I define a function to calculate the expected fitness of a TPC under a given body temperature distribution. I do this using the [Vasseur et al. (2014)](https://royalsocietypublishing.org/rspb/article/281/1779/20132612/77056) approach, which is based on integrating the product of the TPC and the body temperature distribution across all possible body temperatures. ::: {.cell code-description='Defining function to calculate expected fitness'} ```{.r .cell-code} exp_fitness <- function(topt, ctmin, ctmax, mean_tb, sd_tb, skew_tb) { if (sd_tb == 0) { tpc(mean_tb, topt, ctmin, ctmax) } else { # function to calculate integrand integrand <- function(temps) { tpc(temps, topt, ctmin, ctmax) * gen_tb_distrib(temps, mean_tb, sd_tb, skew_tb) # nolint } # integrate integrate(integrand, lower = -30, upper = 60)$value } } # example usage exp_fitness(topt = 25, ctmin = 10, ctmax = 35, mean_tb = 25, sd_tb = 5, skew_tb = 0.5) # nolint ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 0.748758 ``` ::: ::: Lastly, I define a function to calculate the risk of suffering a thermal injury, that is experiencing a body temperature below CTmin or above CTmax. This function is based on calculating the cumulative density of the body temperature distribution at CTmin and CTmax. ::: {.cell code-description='Defining function to calculate thermal injury risk'} ```{.r .cell-code} ptinj <- function(ctmin, topt, ctmax, mean_tb, sd_tb, skew_tb) { if (sd_tb == 0) { if (mean_tb < ctmin || mean_tb > ctmax) { 1 } else { 0 } } else { # estimate the parameters of the skew-normal distribution params <- cp2dp(c(mean_tb, sd_tb, skew_tb), "SN") # generate a sequence of body temperatures and calculate the density dsn(temps, xi = params[1], omega = params[2], alpha = params[3]) # calculate ptinj ptinj_lower <- psn(ctmin, xi = params[1], omega = params[2], alpha = params[3]) # nolint ptinj_upper <- 1 - psn(ctmax, xi = params[1], omega = params[2], alpha = params[3]) # nolint # return sum of both tails ptinj_lower + ptinj_upper } } # example usage ptinj(ctmin = 10, topt = 25, ctmax = 35, mean_tb = 25, sd_tb = 5, skew_tb = 0.5) # nolint ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 0.03556083 ``` ::: ::: ## Estimating body temperature distribution parameters from TPC shape Below, I develop a pseudo-MCMC optimization algorithm to estimate the mean, sd, and skewness of the body temperature distribution that maximizes expected fitness while minimizing the risk of thermal injury. This is just a quick and dirty implementation to explore the idea, so it is not meant to be a rigorous optimization algorithm. ::: {.cell code-description='MCMC optimization to estimate body temperature distribution parameters'} ```{.r .cell-code} # define TPC shape topt <- 30 ctmin <- 5 ctmax <- 36 # define number of iterations n_it <- 2000 n_burn <- 1000 # define objects to store mean_tb, sd_tb, and skew_tb values mean_tbs <- numeric(n_it) sd_tbs <- numeric(n_it) skew_tbs <- numeric(n_it) # set initial values mean_tb_init <- topt sd_tb_init <- 5 skew_tb_init <- 0 # initialize MCMC chain mean_tbs[1] <- mean_tb_init sd_tbs[1] <- sd_tb_init skew_tbs[1] <- skew_tb_init # run MCMC optimization for (i in 2:n_it) { # propose a new value for mean_tb mean_tb_prop <- rnorm(1, mean_tb_init, 5) mean_tb_prop <- ifelse(mean_tb_prop < ctmin, ctmin + 0.1, ifelse(mean_tb_prop > ctmax, ctmax - 0.1, mean_tb_prop)) # nolint # check if value is accepted accept <- rbinom(1, 1, 0.234) exp_fit_current <- exp_fitness(topt, ctmin, ctmax, mean_tbs[i - 1], sd_tbs[i - 1], skew_tbs[i - 1]) # nolint exp_fit_prop <- exp_fitness(topt, ctmin, ctmax, mean_tb_prop, sd_tbs[i - 1], skew_tbs[i - 1]) # nolint ptinj_current <- ptinj(ctmin, topt, ctmax, mean_tbs[i - 1], sd_tbs[i - 1], skew_tbs[i - 1]) # nolint ptinj_prop <- ptinj(ctmin, topt, ctmax, mean_tb_prop, sd_tbs[i - 1], skew_tbs[i - 1]) # nolint # accept or reject proposal if (ptinj_prop < ptinj_current && exp_fit_prop > exp_fit_current && accept == 1) { # nolint mean_tbs[i] <- mean_tb_prop sd_tbs[i] <- sd_tbs[i - 1] skew_tbs[i] <- skew_tbs[i - 1] } else { mean_tbs[i] <- mean_tbs[i - 1] + rnorm(1, 0, 0.1) mean_tbs[i] <- ifelse(mean_tbs[i] < ctmin, ctmin + 0.1, ifelse(mean_tbs[i] > ctmax, ctmax - 0.1, mean_tbs[i])) # nolint sd_tbs[i] <- sd_tbs[i - 1] * runif(1, 0.5, 1.5) sd_tbs[i] <- ifelse(sd_tbs[i] < 1, 1, sd_tbs[i]) skew_tbs[i] <- skew_tbs[i - 1] + runif(1, -0.01, 0.01) ifelse(skew_tbs[i] < -0.99, skew_tbs[i] <- -0.99, skew_tbs[i]) ifelse(skew_tbs[i] > 0.99, skew_tbs[i] <- 0.99, skew_tbs[i]) } # propose a new value for sd_tb sd_tb_prop <- 1 + rexp(1, 1) # check if value is accepted accept <- rbinom(1, 1, 0.234) exp_fit_current <- exp_fitness(topt, ctmin, ctmax, mean_tbs[i], sd_tbs[i - 1], skew_tbs[i - 1]) # nolint exp_fit_prop <- exp_fitness(topt, ctmin, ctmax, mean_tbs[i], sd_tb_prop, skew_tbs[i - 1]) # nolint ptinj_current <- ptinj(ctmin, topt, ctmax, mean_tbs[i], sd_tbs[i - 1], skew_tbs[i - 1]) # nolint ptinj_prop <- ptinj(ctmin, topt, ctmax, mean_tbs[i], sd_tb_prop, skew_tbs[i - 1]) # nolint # accept or reject proposal if (ptinj_prop < ptinj_current & exp_fit_prop > exp_fit_current & accept == 1) { # nolint mean_tbs[i] <- mean_tbs[i] sd_tbs[i] <- sd_tb_prop skew_tbs[i] <- skew_tbs[i - 1] } else { mean_tbs[i] <- mean_tbs[i] + rnorm(1, 0, 0.1) mean_tbs[i] <- ifelse(mean_tbs[i] < ctmin, ctmin + 0.1, ifelse(mean_tbs[i] > ctmax, ctmax - 0.1, mean_tbs[i])) # nolint sd_tbs[i] <- sd_tbs[i - 1] * runif(1, 0.5, 1.5) sd_tbs[i] <- ifelse(sd_tbs[i] < 1, 1, sd_tbs[i]) skew_tbs[i] <- skew_tbs[i - 1] + runif(1, -0.01, 0.01) ifelse(skew_tbs[i] < -0.99, skew_tbs[i] <- -0.99, ifelse(skew_tbs[i] > 0.99, skew_tbs[i] <- 0.99, skew_tbs[i])) # nolint } # propose a new value for skew_tb skew_tb_prop <- runif(1, -0.99, 0.99) # check if value is accepted accept <- rbinom(1, 1, 0.234) exp_fit_current <- exp_fitness(topt, ctmin, ctmax, mean_tbs[i], sd_tbs[i], skew_tbs[i - 1]) # nolint exp_fit_prop <- exp_fitness(topt, ctmin, ctmax, mean_tbs[i], sd_tbs[i], skew_tb_prop) # nolint ptinj_current <- ptinj(ctmin, topt, ctmax, mean_tbs[i], sd_tbs[i], skew_tbs[i - 1]) # nolint ptinj_prop <- ptinj(ctmin, topt, ctmax, mean_tbs[i], sd_tbs[i], skew_tb_prop) # nolint # accept or reject proposal if (ptinj_prop < ptinj_current && exp_fit_prop > exp_fit_current && accept == 1) { # nolint mean_tbs[i] <- mean_tbs[i] sd_tbs[i] <- sd_tbs[i] skew_tbs[i] <- skew_tb_prop } else { mean_tbs[i] <- mean_tbs[i - 1] + rnorm(1, 0, 0.1) mean_tbs[i] <- ifelse(mean_tbs[i] < ctmin, ctmin + 0.1, ifelse(mean_tbs[i] > ctmax, ctmax - 0.1, mean_tbs[i])) # nolint sd_tbs[i] <- sd_tbs[i - 1] * runif(1, 0.5, 1.5) sd_tbs[i] <- ifelse(sd_tbs[i] < 1, 1, sd_tbs[i]) skew_tbs[i] <- skew_tbs[i - 1] + runif(1, -0.01, 0.01) ifelse(skew_tbs[i] < -0.99, skew_tbs[i] <- -0.99, ifelse(skew_tbs[i] > 0.99, skew_tbs[i] <- 0.99, skew_tbs[i])) # nolint } } ``` ::: Next, I plot the MCMC chains and the posterior distributions of the mean, sd, and skewness parameters of the body temperature distribution. ::: {.cell code-description='Plotting MCMC chains and posterior distributions'} ```{.r .cell-code} # plot posteriors of mean_tb, sd_tb, and skew_tb tibble(mean_tb = mean_tbs, sd_tb = sd_tbs, skew_tb = skew_tbs) |> mutate(iteration = row_number()) |> pivot_longer(cols = -c(iteration), names_to = "parameter", values_to = "value") |> # nolint ggplot(aes(x = iteration, y = value)) + geom_line() + facet_wrap(~ parameter, scales = "free") + labs(x = "Iteration", y = "Value") + theme_minimal() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/plot-chains-and-posteriors-1.png){width=1800} ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # plot posterior distributions of mean_tb, sd_tb, and skew_tb tibble(mean_tb = mean_tbs, sd_tb = sd_tbs, skew_tb = skew_tbs) |> mutate(iteration = row_number()) |> filter(iteration > n_burn) |> # discard burn-in select(-iteration) |> pivot_longer(cols = everything(), names_to = "parameter", values_to = "value") |> # nolint ggplot(aes(x = value)) + geom_density(fill = "lightblue") + facet_wrap(~ parameter, scales = "free") + labs(x = "Value", y = "Density") + theme_minimal() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/plot-chains-and-posteriors-2.png){width=1800} ::: ::: In many of the test runs I did, the MCM algorithm swings too much, specially when estimating the mean. I have to think about how to make this as efficient as possible. And finally, in the classic Bayesian fashion, I summarize the posterior distributions of the mean, sd, and skewness parameters of the body temperature distribution by calculating their means and 95% confidence intervals. ::: {.cell code-description='Summarizing posterior distributions of body temperature distribution parameters'} ```{.r .cell-code} # get average and 95% confidence interval of tb distribution parameters posterior <- tibble(mean_tb = mean_tbs, sd_tb = sd_tbs, skew_tb = skew_tbs) |> mutate(iteration = row_number()) |> filter(iteration > n_burn) |> # discard burn-in select(-iteration) |> pivot_longer( cols = everything(), names_to = "parameter", values_to = "value" ) |> group_by(parameter) |> summarise( mean = mean(value), lower = quantile(value, 0.025), upper = quantile(value, 0.975) ) kbl( posterior |> rename( `Parameter` = parameter, `Mean` = mean, `95% CI Lower` = lower, `95% CI Upper` = upper ) ) |> kable_styling(full_width = FALSE) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Parameter Mean 95% CI Lower 95% CI Upper
mean_tb 25.9997698 23.1570019 30.1976243
sd_tb 2.7667919 1.0000000 12.6767780
skew_tb -0.7504404 -0.9813645 -0.6024644
````` ::: ::: Just to get a sense of the predicted body temperature distribution, I plot the TPC and the predicted body temperature distribution based on the mean values of the parameters estimated by the MCMC algorithm. ::: {.cell code-description='Plotting TPC and predicted body temperature distribution'} ```{.r .cell-code} # plot predicted body temperature distribution and TPC temps <- seq(0, 50, by = 0.1) tibble( temps = temps, tpc_values = tpc(temps, topt, ctmin, ctmax), tb_density_mean = gen_tb_distrib( temps, posterior$mean[1], posterior$mean[2], posterior$mean[3] ) ) |> ggplot(aes(x = temps)) + geom_area(aes(y = tb_density_mean * 2), fill = "red", alpha = 0.5) + geom_line(aes(y = tpc_values), color = "black", linewidth = 1.5) + geom_vline(xintercept = topt, linetype = "dashed", color = "black") + geom_vline( xintercept = posterior$mean[1], linetype = "dashed", color = "red" ) + coord_cartesian(xlim = c(ctmin - 5, ctmax + 5)) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/plot-tpc-and-tb-distribs-1.png){width=1800} ::: :::
